coredata.dc:title: Redox-initiated polymerization of N-vinylcarbazole based on carbon dots for modification and beyond
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Chenghao
Sun, Jingye
Sana, Ulla
Ma, Yongjun
Ren, Xianyan
Liu, Cailin
coredata.dc:description: Herein, carbon dots (CDs) and N,N-dimethylaniline (DMA) were designed as a redox couple for redox-initiated polymerization of N-vinylcarbazole (NVC) under mild conditions. Several ratios of CDs to DMA and various temperatures were adopted to prove the feasibilities of the redox-initiated polymerization. Meanwhile, the CDs as fillers were encapsulated, crosslinked and passivated by the resulting poly (N-vinylcarbazole) (PVK) to form red emissive PVK-CDs composites with a fluorescence quantum yield up to 28.75%, which is significantly higher than that of pristine CDs. In particular, the loading of CDs also enhanced the electron-transfer properties and thermostability of PVK. Hence, this work not only applied CDs to redox-initiated polymerization of NVC so as to prepare CDs modified PVK, but also illustrated the electron-transfer properties of CDs in polymerization and PVK modification. Expectedly, by taking advantages of electron-transfer properties of CDs, it is expectable for redox-initiated polymerization to synthesize more CDs modified polymers for promising applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Poly (N-vinylcarbazole)
Redox couple
Electron-transfer properties
Redox-initiated polymerization

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of PVK-CDs by our designed redox couple
Construction of the CDs/DMA redox couple
Possible mechanism of electron-transfer properties of CDs
The structural characterization of PVK-CDs composites
The performances of PVK-CDs compared with commercial PVK

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The illustration of polymerization process initiated by CDs/DMA redox couple.
Yield of PVK-CDs at (a) different mass ratio of CDs/DMA and (b) different temperatures. Insert photo: collected products under different conditions.
Tentative mechanism for in-situ polymerization of NVC and illustration for electron-transfer process of CDs.
(a) General TEM picture of PVK-CDs. (b) Partial enlarged TEM picture of CDs embedded in PVK matrices.

(a) UV–vis spectra of NVC, commercial PVK and PVK-CDs. Insert: UV–vis spectra of NP and CDs (b) PL spectra of PVK-CDs. Insert: the fluorescence photos of CDs and PVK-CDs. (c) FTIR spectra of commercial PVK, CDs and PVK-CDs. (d) 1HNMR spectra of commercial PVK and PVK-CDs prepared at 30 °C and 70 °C, respectively.

(a) CV curves of prepared PVK-CDs and commercial PVK. (b) EIS Nyquist plots of PVK-CDs prepared at different temperatures compared with commercial PVK. (c) The line of ω−1/2 ∼ Z′ of all samples. (d) TGA thermograms of PVK-CDs, commercial PVK and CDs under N2 at a heating rate of 20 °C/min. Insert: DTG curves of prepared PVK-CDs commercial PVK and CDs.
Effect on yield of PVK-CDs from different usage of CDs.
Effect on yield of PVK-CDs from different preparing temperatures.
The impedance resistance of PVK and PVK-CDs prepared at different temperatures.

head.ce:title: Redox-initiated polymerization of N-vinylcarbazole based on carbon dots for modification and beyond

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Southwest University of Science and Technology, Mianyang, 621010, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering
Southwest University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Southwest University of Science and Technology, Mianyang, 621010, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, carbon dots (CDs) and N,N-dimethylaniline (DMA) were designed as a redox couple for redox-initiated polymerization of N-vinylcarbazole (NVC) under mild conditions. Several ratios of CDs to DMA and various temperatures were adopted to prove the feasibilities of the redox-initiated polymerization. Meanwhile, the CDs as fillers were encapsulated, crosslinked and passivated by the resulting poly (N-vinylcarbazole) (PVK) to form red emissive PVK-CDs composites with a fluorescence quantum yield up to 28.75%, which is significantly higher than that of pristine CDs. In particular, the loading of CDs also enhanced the electron-transfer properties and thermostability of PVK. Hence, this work not only applied CDs to redox-initiated polymerization of NVC so as to prepare CDs modified PVK, but also illustrated the electron-transfer properties of CDs in polymerization and PVK modification. Expectedly, by taking advantages of electron-transfer properties of CDs, it is expectable for redox-initiated polymerization to synthesize more CDs modified polymers for promising applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Poly (N-vinylcarbazole) (PVK) is a remarkable polymer that deserves a good nature of film forming and a high glass transition temperature about 220 °C with stable structures of carbazole segments [1]. Owing to excellent conductivity and good machinability, PVK has been extensively applied in the photovoltaic sector or electronic field [2,3]. Even in antimicrobial applications, PVK provides nanomaterials with matrices for embedding otherwise nanomaterials are hard to make effects alone [4,5]. As a result, apart from keeping pristine advantages, PVK is also expected to get enhancements in many aspects by introducing various external materials.

PVK is usually prepared by thermal initiation, which may involve thermal initiator at high temperature [6–8]. To this end, the polymerization can also be realized by taking advantages of electron transfer between metals ions and monomers with specific reaction conditions such as photoinitiation in order to induce monomers to produce radicals more effectively [9–13]. Nevertheless, some critical issues exist such as that metal ions as initiators are either expensive or hazardous to both environment and humans, which are not negligible. Hence, it is important to regulate reaction or initiation conditions so as to optimize the polymerization process.

In order to seek a facile method that is free from metal ions to prepare PVK, carbon materials are ideal candidates that can also be adopted to functionalize PVK. For example, carbon nanotubes show the potential as initiators owing to their excellent electron-transfer properties with the activation of heat [14,15], and what's more, the presence of carbon nanotubes in PVK enhance their photoconductivity so that the resulting nanocomposites have great possibilities in photovoltaic sectors.

In this case, zero-dimensional carbon dots (CDs) emerge as a nova among carbon nanomaterials in scientific fields due to their low toxicity, facile preparation and environmental friendliness. More importantly, CDs also exhibit both electron-accepting and electron-donating properties that are beneficial to the generations of radicals so as to initiate the polymerization [16,17]. Besides, CDs together with other metal oxide or oxidizing agents are capable of improving the ability of separating and transferring electrons when they participate in redox process (e.g. Fenton-like reaction) [18]. Just like electron reservoirs, CDs with large π-conjugated structures are prone to transfer electrons, playing a similar role as traditional metal quantum dots in some respects. Additionally, CDs with electron-accepting and electron-donating properties can improve the charge transfer efficiency of PVK as photovoltaic materials [19].

Not only can CDs be introduced to tune the polymerization process and modify the resulting polymer, but CDs can also be passivated by the resulting polymer at the same time. Taking the effort to passivate CDs using polymer as an example, poly (vinylalcohol) (PVA) was employed as passivation agents to enhance the emission of the CDs [20]. In epoxy resin, the resin matrices render CDs to keep close but avoid aggregation, enabling electrons to transfer effectively [21]. Similarly, it is acceptable to modify PVK using CDs to boost the electron-transfer efficiency. Especially under mild conditions, CDs tend to be potential candidates to initiate the polymerization.

To sum up, considering that many polymerization processes of PVK require high temperature, light or the assistance of metal ions, we proposed a novel CDs/N,N-dimethylaniline (DMA) redox couple to prepare PVK even under mild condition. Furthermore, CDs also make a positive impact on electron-transfer process and induce radical generation of monomers to form PVK-CDs composites. Expectedly, the prepared PVK-CDs composites would get enhanced compared with conventional PVK or pristine CDs.

Pyrene and poly (N-vinlycarbazole) (PVK) were purchased from Macklin (Shanghai). N-vinlycarbazole (NVC), N,N-dimethylaniline (DMA), and tetrabutylammonium hexafluorophosphate (TBAPF6) were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai). Toluene, cyclohexane, methanol, acetonitrile (ACN), sulfuric acid (H2SO4) and nitric acid (HNO3) were obtained from Kelong (Chengdu). All the reagents were analytically pure and used without further purification.

Nitropyrene (NP) was synthesized via typical acid treatment ever reported with a slight modification [22]. Briefly, 2.02 g (0.01 mol) pyrene was weighted and shifted to a round bottom flask. 120 mL HNO3 and 40 mL H2SO4 were added in sequence. The mixture was kept stirring and refluxing at 80 °C for 48 h. After cooling down to room temperature naturally and filtrating subsequently, the precipitation was collected and washed to neutral using deionized water and got dried in a vacuum oven. Finally, the yellowish powder was obtained.

CDs were prepared by sacrificing NP as carbon source via solvothermal method. 0.4 g NP was firstly dispersed in 40 mL toluene and underwent ultrasonication for 30 min. The suspension was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave, which was kept at 180 °C for 12 h in an oven. After solvothermal process, a 0.22 μm membrane was used to remove the insoluble impurities. The resulting dark red solution was subsequently concentrated and dried to obtain the CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of PVK-CDs by our designed redox couple
ce:section.ce:para: 1.00 g NVC was dissolved in 10 mL toluene and maintained vigorous stirring in a round-bottomed flask. CDs were dispersed in 5 mL toluene and 20 mg DMA was dissolved in another 5 mL toluene. Hereafter, CDs and DMA were added dropwise to the flask separately. After the addition, the temperature was raised to 30–80 °C and kept for 8 h. Finally, toluene was evaporated in a rotary evaporator, and the concentrated mixture was precipitated and washed times using 25 mL hot cyclohexane and 25 mL hot methanol in sequence to remove excess CDs and NVC. Thereafter, the solid PVK-CDs were collected.

Morphologies of PVK-CDs without staining were observed by FEI Tecnai G2 F20 high resolution transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Fourier Transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded by Bruker Tensor 27. The U-3900 spectrophotometer and LS55 fluorescence spectrometer were used to obtain ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectra and photoluminescence (PL) spectra, respectively. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was used to perform thermal stability measurements of the prepared PVK-CDs, which were heated from 20 °C to 800 °C at a heating rate of 20 °C/min on NETZSCH TG 209F1 Libra.

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurements were performed to investigate the electron-transfer properties and mechanism. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was applied to study the ion-transport and dielectric/impedance properties. Above electrochemical characterizations were carried out on a CHI 760 E using a three-electrode setup in a solution of 0.1 mol/L TBAPF6 dissolved in ACN, where PVK-CDs coated FTO glass was applied as the working electrode, Pt tablet as the counter electrode and Ag/Ag+ as the reference electrode, respectively.

As a matter of fact, most CDs were embedded in the polymer and formed the composites named as PVK-CDs that were collected after purification though some CDs along with unreacted monomers had been removed after washing process. As a result, the yield could be better calculated following the equation:
Y i e l d =
m
m 0
+
m 1
× 100 %

Here, m
1 , m
0 and m represent the mass of input CDs, NVC and the obtained purified PVK-CDs, respectively. All experiments in various temperatures were repeated three times and the data were recorded by mean values from three trials.

Briefly, rhodamine B in ethanol as a standard of which literature quantum yield (QY) is 0.5 [23]. The measurement was carried out following a widely accepted method and results were calculated as the equation below [24]:
Q Y = Q
Y s
(
F
F s
)
(
A s
A
)
(
η
η s
)
2
Where, QY, F, A, η are the quantum yield, the measured integrated emission intensity, the absorbance and the refractive index, respectively. The subscript “s” refers to the standard fluorophore of known quantum yield. In order to minimize reabsorption effects absorbencies in the 10 mm fluorescence cuvette were kept under 0.05 at the excitation wavelength (500 nm).

After solvothermal treatment, NP was carbonized to CDs that possess abundant nitrogen-containing groups and are rich in nitrogen-containing aromatic structures. Besides, the large conjugated structures are beneficial to improve properties in reserving and transferring electrons. To make use of properties, we conceived an idea that CDs and DMA were used as a redox couple to initiate the free radical polymerization of NVC under mild conditions, and meanwhile CDs as nano materials improve the performances of the PVK by forming PVK-CDs composites in situ, as illustrated in Fig. 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of the CDs/DMA redox couple
ce:section.ce:para: When taking CDs and DMA as a redox couple, the mass ratio of CDs to DMA is critical to the polymerization, the CDs play a more important role in transferring electron for radical generations, thus their usage affects the efficiency of polymerization significantly. A control experiment without DMA was firstly performed at 60 °C for 8 h and barely any product was collected, illustrating that not enough free radical was generated for polymerization. Then different ratios between CDs and DMA are designed to clarify the effect of CDs in the CDs/DMA redox couple and the success of the couple in initiating the polymerization of NVC, which are listed in Table 1 . As what is depicted, almost no product was collected in absence of CDs, suggesting that few radicals were generated with only DMA so that the polymerization is hard to proceed at 60 °C. And the yield is quite low when CDs are insufficient compared to DMA which may result from quenching in some generated radicals by excess DMA. Notably, when the mass ratio between CDs and DMA is increased from 2:1 to 3:1 the yield increases dramatically from 36.54% to 89.05% and keeps about 90% as the mass of CDs continuously increase (Fig. 2 a), proving the significance of CDs in constructing the redox couple with DMA for the polymerization of NVC.

With enough CDs in the redox couple, the redox-initiated polymerization of NVC could be carried out at low temperature. Table 2 shows the yield of resulting products at the temperature range of 30–70 °C, which could be deduced that CDs/DMA redox couple does good to generate and transfer radicals to monomers, thereby promoting the polymerization of NVC. Even at a temperature as low as 30 °C, 531.1 mg product is collected which further proves the feasibility of CDs/DMA as a redox couple, though the yield at low temperature is relatively low. At higher temperature, radicals are generated faster and meanwhile the polymerization is boosted, thereby leading to a higher yield. In Fig. 2a and b, the inserted photos also show the collected products initiated by CDs/DMA redox couple for polymerization of NVC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism of electron-transfer properties of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the redox properties of CDs, a mechanism of electron-transfer properties of CDs during polymerization and the effects of CDs on PVK-CDs composites could be deduced, which is shown in Fig. 3 . First of all, it is predictable that CDs incline to accept electrons from the amino nitrogen of DMA due to the electron-drawing effect of –NO2 [22,25], and subsequently the DMA radicals are prone to generate due to the electron transfer from neighbor carbon to amino nitrogen, leading to the radical transfer to NVC and the propagation of PVK backbones. Throughout the process, CDs act as an electron reservoir that would also supply radicals to PVK backbones with electrons for termination and continuously receive electrons from DMA in order to promise the polymerization. CDs provided sites for binding to PVK and participated in crosslink of PVK so that the polymer composites named PVK-CDs came into being. An interaction between the CDs as nanoparticles and PVK as polymer chains develops a restricted segmental region around the CDs [26].

In terms of the electron-transfer behaviors of CDs in PVK-CDs composites, the in-situ produced PVK could functionalize CDs and interact with nitro groups belonging to CDs [27]. In theory, the carbazole moieties in PVK have tendency to donate more electrons to CDs via π-π conjugations and further makes CDs an electron-rich reservoir, which could lead to the stronger fluorescence of PVK passivated CDs than that of CDs without passivation. Besides, the carbazolylium radical cations should be prone to generate and meanwhile CDs are negative charged under applied voltage. In this case, a donor-acceptor structure consisted of PVK and CDs comes into being. In particular, the presence of CDs could further help PVK matrices to become a more efficient current carrier transport and form more stable radical cations, which could easily tune the optical and electro properties of PVK, thereby showing great potential in photo-electronic applications using CDs modified PVK. Therefore, several characterizations including structures, optical properties and electron-transfer properties of PVK-CDs are required in order to understand better.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The structural characterization of PVK-CDs composites
ce:section.ce:para: As a matter of fact, the resulting polymer composites using CDs/DMA redox couple is the PVK-passivated CDs. CDs can play a role both as initiators for NVC and fillers into the produced PVK to form PVK-CDs composites, which can be confirmed by the following morphology, structure and optical characterization of the resulting product.

TEM images firstly reveal the morphologies of CDs in PVK, as is shown in Fig. 4 a and Fig. 4b, CDs are mainly dispersed in PVK matrices and all dots possess a size distribution from 1.59 nm to 4.45 nm with an average size of 2.53 nm. Moreover, the lattice of CDs can be observed and measured about 0.21 nm. On the one hand, the π-π interactions between the carbazole rings in the PVK and aromatic structures in the CDs enable PVK to passivate CDs easily. On the other hand, in spite of electron interactions between CDs and PVK, CDs also possess the same charge and tend to get dispersed rather than get aggregated in PVK matrices. As a result, NVC was successfully polymerized due to the effect of CDs/DMA redox couple and CDs as fillers were encapsulated in PVK passivation layer.

Optical properties of the samples before and after the reaction were measured to investigate more details. In Fig. 5 a, the insert picture referring to the differences between CDs and NP. It can be clearly seen that the absorption band emerges from 450 nm to 600 nm after carbonization, which is attributed to sp
2 domain structures of CDs as a result of the increase of conjugation. As for the absorption of n-π* transitions at 411 nm, it means that CDs still keep the same nitrogen-containing groups as NP. Besides, three results can be obtained from the UV–vis spectra of the NVC, PVK and PVK-CDs. Firstly, the absorption peaks at 327 nm and 341 nm belonging to the n-π* and π-π* transitions in pedant carbazole moieties of PVK appear in the UV–vis spectra of the PVK-CDs. Secondly, compared to the spectrum of the NVC, the intensity of the peaks is decreased in that of the PVK. Thirdly, the peaks in the spectra of PVK-CDs are consistent with that of PVK but with a slight red shift of 1–2 nm. These results show that not only the CDs/DMA redox couple enables NVC to participate in the free radical polymerization, but also the electronic interactions between CDs and PVK lower the energy of transitions.

The PL spectra of PVK-CDs in Fig. 5b show that the PVK-CDs exhibit non-excitation wavelength dependence with a maximum excitation wavelength at 520 nm while emission wavelength is centered at about 605 nm, suggesting that there is only one kind of luminescence center [24]. Furthermore, PVK-CDs exhibit a fluorescence QY of 28.75% while the fluorescence QY of pristine CDs is 19.88% with an enhancement about 44.61% (The details were given in Supporting Information), illustrating that the carbonization helps to form more graphite nitrogen structures in CDs for red emission [28–31]. Besides, in CDs, π electrons in the aromatic structures along with lone-pair electrons belonging to nitrogen incline to delocalize through n-π and π-π interactions in the PVK passivation layer to make a more rigid conformation, thereby making the “core-shell” PVK-CDs highly emissive [32].

To investigate more details of products before and after polymerization, the FTIR spectra of CDs, PVK-CDs and commercial PVK are shown in Fig. 5c. For PVK-CDs, the absorption peaks such as CC stretching vibrations at 1453 cm−1, C–H out-of-plane bending vibrations of carbazole rings at 721 cm−1 and 747 cm−1, as well as C–H stretching vibrations peak of –CH2 at 2926 cm−1, which are consistent with that of the commercial PVK. In the spectra of CDs, there are strong absorption peaks around 1340 cm−1 and 1539 cm−1 corresponding to symmetrical and antisymmetric stretching vibrations of –NO2, respectively. Besides, it can also be seen that CC and CN aromatic stretching vibration peaks appear at 1596 cm−1 and 1452 cm−1 [33], stretching vibrations peak of C–H aromatic bonds appears at 3061 cm−1, as well as multiple C–H out-of-plane bending vibrations peaks of aromatic rings appear at 807 cm−1, 839 cm−1, 873 cm−1 and 912 cm−1. All these peaks of CDs are attributed to the pyridine nitrogen structures that also emerge in the FTIR spectrum of PVK-CDs, illustrating that CDs/DMA successfully initiated the polymerization of NVC and meanwhile CDs were successfully passivated by PVK.

Another persuasive proof came from the comparative study of the 1HNMR spectra of commercial PVK and the PVK-CDs synthesized at 30 °C and 70 °C, respectively. As is shown in Fig. 5d, it is not hard to note that the characteristic broad peak f about δ1∼δ2 belongs to –CH2- and the broad peak e at around δ3.2 represents –CH– in the backbone of PVK. As for sharp proton signal around δ1.5 in PVK-CDs prepared at 30 °C and 70 °C, it should be partially interference of –CH2- in cyclohexane (washing solution) and little trace of water [34]. Notably, the proton signals of –CHCH- in monomers around δ5.2 and δ5.6 disappeared [35]. Their multiple peaks turn to bread peaks owing to the polymerization, which are apparently different from that of monomers, indicating the successful polymerization of NVC. Besides, most essential peaks in synthesized PVK-CDs prepared at low temperature or high temperature are consistent with that in commercial PVK, particularly in segments from δ6 to δ8, peak a, b, c and d are ascribed to protons in carbazole rings [13]. Accordingly, it could come to a conclusion that CDs/DMA redox couple as metal-free initiators could help lower the decomposition temperature of carbon-carbon double bonds so that NVC could be polymerized effectively. The temperature only affects the yield while the polymerization can proceed under mild conditions with the assistance of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The performances of PVK-CDs compared with commercial PVK
ce:section.ce:para: Owing to the CDs/DMA redox couple, the PVK was successfully synthesized under mild conditions, which further achieved the goal of the introduction of CDs into PVK, thereby leading to formation of PVK-CDs composites instead of bare PVK. Taking commercial PVK as a control, in view of the excellent electro and optical performances of CDs, the composites should perform better in transferring electrons as expected.

CV characterizations were firstly implemented in 0.1 mol/L TBAPF6 of ACN at 0.05V/s with a potential scan from 0 to 1.2 V to understand the electron-transfer properties. As is shown in Fig. 6 a, the curves display the electrochemical behaviors of PVK and PVK-CDs. For commercial PVK, the oxidation peak at 0.93 V and reduction peak at 0.51 V can be observed in the voltammograms, which is the signal of carbazolylium radical cations, however, the curves of PVK-CDs prepared at steps of temperatures seem completely different. On the one hand, there is a downshift of reduction peak to an approximate range from 0.25 V to 0.4 V, which may be inferred that CDs are negative charged in the composites. On the other hand, there is merely no clear oxidation peak observed in PVK-CDs, indicating that the doping behaviors of CDs could have a positive impact on transferring electrons to PVK. Consequently, CDs may be considered as amphoteric roles that act as both electron acceptors and electron donors in the system, which not only contribute to initiate the polymerization, but also contribute to transfer electrons in PVK-CDs film.

Moreover, the increase of the peak current of PVK-CDs compared to PVK further approves that CDs improves the electron-transfer properties according to the voltammograms (Fig. 6a). When PVK is fixed to the FTO glass electrode, it would form a thin film that hinders the electron-transfer process [36]. Nevertheless, CDs have possibilities of binding to PVK via electrostatic interactions and π-π conjugations, which weaken the hindering effect but promote electrons to transfer through the film. Consequently, a model of transferring electrons among electrode, polymer and electrolyte layer is likely to be established.

For further discussions of the charge transfer in the interface of PVK-CDs film, EIS characterization was also utilized. The impedance spectra of PVK and PVK-CDs were presented, and the appropriate equivalent circuit fitting to data was also done in Fig. 6b, where the Rs is the solution resistance and Rct is the charge transfer resistance. Besides, Cdl represents the double layer capacity while Zw represents Warburg impedance ascribed to diffusion-controlled mass transfer. At low frequencies, the film may be estimated to exhibit capacitive behaviors of the faradaic pseudocapacitance [37], and what's more, the slope of PVK-CDs is sharper as the increase of prepared temperature, indicating that the impendence of the interface diffusion decreases. Furthermore, though the curves are in similar shapes at high frequencies, the diameters of semicircles corresponding to electron-transfer limited process among series of PVK-CDs and commercial PVK are different, namely the formation of PVK composites occurred and meanwhile CDs enhanced the charge-transfer abilities of PVK.

As is presented in Table 3 , the real impedance values of PVK-CDs film are slightly less than that of PVK, which may be due that trace amounts of CDs enhance interactions of π-π conjugations in PVK and the transfer charge is prone to flow through the film smoothly. In other words, charge-transfer ability can be primarily estimated by Rct because commercial PVK with the Rct of 70.4 Ω is larger than that of all PVK-CDs, which can be inferred that the presence of CDs promotes charge transfer in PVK-CDs. Taking both shifting of the reduction peaks in CV and the decrease of impedance resistance in EIS into consideration, a conclusion that CDs play the part of either electron donors or electron acceptors could be deduced.

To better understand the differences of PVK with and without CDs, the diffusion coefficient can be calculated to estimate charge-transfer properties based on the following equation:
D =
R 2
T 2
2
n 4
A 2
F 4
C 2
σ 2
Where n, R, T, F, A, C and D represent the number of electrons per molecule, gas constant, absolute temperature in the experiment, Faraday constant, diffusion coefficient, electrode area and ions concentration of electrolyte, respectively. Besides, σ is Warburg factor which has the relationship with Z’. As the line of ω−1/2 ∼ Z′ in Fig. 6c, the slope defined as σ can be easily calculated by fitting curves and it is evident that a sharper slope usually corresponds to a smaller diffusion coefficient according to the equation. The diffusion coefficient of PVK is about 8.27 × 10−13 cm2 s−1 while the diffusion coefficient dramatically increases in all PVK-CDs samples owing to the encapsulation of CDs. Hence, we can see that the presence of CDs may strengthen the charge-transfer efficiency and ions penetration rate by affecting the structure of PVK film according to that the slopes of PVK-CDs are obviously sharper than that of PVK at low frequencies. In other words, CDs act as both stations and antennas that enable electrons to accommodate and transport.

Besides, TGA is a useful method to evaluate the thermal properties of prepared PVK-CDs. As illustrated in Fig. 5d, two considerable weight loss steps of PVK-CDs mainly occur at 200–400 °C (30%) and 400–500 °C (45%). After undergoing the polymerization, some solvent molecules, unreacted monomers, and small PVK chains with low molecular weights would remain in prepared products. Therefore, as other literature reported, the reason accounting for the first drop-off in TGA should be the evaporation of small molecules mentioned above [38,39], as well as the weight loss of CDs that could also be observed in bare CDs. The subsequent weight loss stage could be observed around 450 °C, attributing to thermal fracture of PVK chains and decomposition of their backbone, which proves that CDs boost the formation of PVK from another perspective. More interestingly, there is no complete weight loss over 500 °C and retained weight of all prepared samples still keep about 10%, indicating that the presence of CDs lead to a less weight loss as a result of the higher composition of more degradable PVK at the temperature range of 400–500 °C. In addition, from the observation of DTG curves, the peak temperature of all the prepared PVK-CDs shift towards higher temperature compared to commercial PVK, which may be ascribed to the crosslink formation of CDs and PVK so as to offer a high strength of bonding [40]. Accordingly, it is predictable that the loading of CDs improves the thermostability of PVK.

CDs prepared from NP not only kept abundant oxidizing groups and good oil-dispersibility, but also exhibited excellent electron-accepting and electron-donating properties so that CDs along with DMA showed good potential in constructing redox initiating system and promised the redox-initiated free radical polymerization of NVC under the mild conditions. Even at 30 °C, the NVC was successfully initiated and the yield of the polymer composites was about 50.1%, and the yield can be increased up to 90% at higher temperature. More importantly, the prepared products named PVK-CDs are the composites of PVK modified by CDs rather than bare PVK, and the CDs act as both initiators and fillers to crosslink with PVK. Besides, the composites were prepared in situ without external thermal initiator.

Benefiting from the electron interactions between PVK and CDs that confirmed according to electrochemical analysis, PVK-CDs possess excellent optical properties and good electron-transfer properties. The mechanism was also illustrated that CDs not only induce radical generations of NVC, but also enhance the electron-transfer abilities of the obtained modified PVK. Furthermore, the presence of CDs is beneficial to the thermostability of the PVK-CDs.

In conclusion, this work proposed a novel way of initiating polymerization in order to prepare PVK by applying CDs and DMA in redox initiating system, and meanwhile provided an inspiration for taking advantages of electron-transfer properties of CDs to enhance PVK and beyond. Based on the unique properties of CDs, it is also expected to develop more initiation routes from monomers to polymer and to design CDs incorporated polymer for promising applications.

Chenghao Li: Data curation, Writing – original draft. Jingye Sun: Conceptualization, Investigation. Ulla Sana: Validation. Yongjun Ma: Methodology. Xianyan Ren: Project administration, Writing – review & editing. Cailin Liu: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the assistance from Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for taking TEM pictures. This work is supported by the Science and Technology Plan Project of Sichuan Province [2021YJ0348], Southwest University of Science and Technology of the Startup Foundation for Doctors [16ZX7139] and the Longshan Talent Program [17LZX675, 18LZXT01].

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.polymer.2022.124601.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Stereo-chemical contributions to the glass transition and liquid-liquid phase separation in high molecular weight poly(N-vinyl carbazole)

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.G. Al Lafi, J.N. Hay. Stereo-chemical contributions to the glass transition and liquid-liquid phase separation in high molecular weight poly(N-vinyl carbazole). RSC Adv. 6 (2016) 29326-29333.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T.M. Fulghum, P. Taranekar, R.C. Advincula. Grafting hole-transport precursor polymer brushes on ITO electrodes: surface-initiated polymerization and conjugated polymer network formation of PVK. Macromolecules 41 (2008) 5681-5687.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Yuan, B. Han, T. Fang, Q. Shan, J. Song. Flat, luminescent, and defect-less perovskite films on PVK for light-emitting diodes with enhanced efficiency and stability. ACS Appl. Electron. Mater. 2 (2020) 3530-3537.
C.M. Santos, M.C. Tria, R.A. Vergara, F. Ahmed, R.C. Advincula, D.F. Rodrigues. Antimicrobial graphene polymer (PVK-GO) nanocomposite films. Chem. Commun. 47 (2011) 8892-8894.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Ahmed, C.M. Santos, R.A. Vergara, M.C. Tria, R. Advincula, D.F. Rodrigues. Antimicrobial applications of electroactive PVK-SWNT nanocomposites. Environ. Sci. Technol. 46 (2012) 1804-1810.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H.X. Wu, X.Q. Qiu, R.F. Cai, S.X. Qian. Poly(N-vinyl carbazole)-grafted multiwalled carbon nanotubes: Synthesis via direct free radical reaction and optical limiting properties. Appl. Surf. Sci. 253 (2007) 5122-5128.
